scenarios.title: Morning Transition
scenarios.context: Students are entering the room after recess. Several students are loud, and one student is refusing to sit down.
scenarios.behavior_prompt: A student says, 'I don't want to do this assignment,' while two others begin laughing and copying the behavior.
scenarios.learning_goal: Practice calm redirection, clear expectations, and positive classroom management.

response_options.text: Calmly move closer, privately redirect the student, and restate the expected task.
response_options.feedback: Strong choice. This preserves dignity, reduces audience attention, and brings the class back toward the learning task.

response_options.text: Call the student out loudly in front of the class and warn them immediately.
response_options.feedback: This may stop the behavior temporarily, but it can escalate embarrassment and increase peer attention.

response_options.text: Ignore the behavior and continue the lesson without addressing it.
response_options.feedback: Ignoring may allow the behavior to spread. Some behaviors need a calm, quick response to protect the classroom environment.

scenarios.title: Group Work Conflict
scenarios.context: Students are working in groups on a technology activity. One group begins arguing over who gets to use the computer.
scenarios.behavior_prompt: Two students raise their voices. One student pushes the keyboard away and says, 'You never let me do anything.'
scenarios.learning_goal: Practice conflict resolution, equitable participation, and safe technology use.

response_options.text: Pause the group, assign roles, and use a timer so each student gets a fair turn.
response_options.feedback: Strong choice. Clear roles and time limits reduce conflict while keeping the group focused on the task.

response_options.text: Take the computer away from the group for the rest of class.
response_options.feedback: This may stop the conflict, but it removes the learning opportunity instead of teaching students how to collaborate.

response_options.text: Pick one student to control the computer because they are usually responsible.
response_options.feedback: This can seem unfair and may increase frustration. A structured rotation would better support collaboration.
